title: Young offenders
summary: 9084 Criminal — youth court, referral orders, YROs, and detention for under-18s.
learningObjectives: Outline referral orders and youth rehabilitation orders.
Evaluate welfare principle and age of criminal responsibility.

simpleExplanation.title: Justice for under-18s
simpleExplanation.summary: Young offenders are dealt with in youth court with sentences focused on rehabilitation — referral orders, YROs, and DTOs replace many adult disposals.
steps: DTO — custody for serious youth offences.

flashcards.back: Defendants aged 10–17 at date of offence — those under 10 cannot be criminally responsible.
Mandatory for most first-time young offenders pleading guilty — panel agrees contract with reparation and intervention.
Community sentence with requirements (curfew, supervision, education, unpaid work if 16+).
Custodial sentence for 12–17 year-olds — half served in detention, half in community under supervision.
Children Act 1989 — court must consider child's welfare; youth sentencing prioritises rehabilitation.
Serious cases (e.g. homicide, grave crimes) may be sent to Crown Court; most offences stay in youth court.

flashcards.front: What is the age of criminal responsibility in England and Wales?
flashcards.back: 10 years old. Children under 10 are considered 'doli incapax' (incapable of wrong) and cannot be prosecuted for a criminal offence.

flashcards.front: What is the principal aim of the youth justice system?
flashcards.back: As stated in the Crime and Disorder Act 1998, the principal aim is 'to prevent offending by children and young persons'. This is a forward-looking, preventative aim, distinct from the mixed aims (e.g. retribution) of the adult system.

flashcards.front: What is a Youth Rehabilitation Order (YRO)?
flashcards.back: A flexible community sentence for young offenders where a court can choose from 18 different requirements (e.g., curfew, supervision) to create a tailored sentence.
A sentence for most first-time young offenders who plead guilty. They are referred to a Youth Offender Panel to agree a contract of rehabilitative and restorative activities.

flashcards.front: Describe a Detention and Training Order (DTO).
flashcards.back: A custodial sentence for 12-17 year olds, lasting 4-24 months. Half is served in custody and the second half is served under supervision in the community.

sections.content: **Young offenders** (2.3.2) — emphasise **welfare** and **rehabilitation** over punishment. Compare disposals with **adult sentences** (2.3.1) — many adult options are unavailable or modified for under-18s.
The Youth Court: Jurisdiction and Procedure

The Youth Court is a specialist branch of the Magistrates' Court dealing with most offences committed by young people aged 10 to 17. Its primary aim, established by the Crime and Disorder Act 1998, is to prevent offending. The environment is less formal than an adult court to reduce intimidation; for example, judges and lawyers do not wear wigs and gowns. The public is excluded, and reporting restrictions automatically apply to protect the identity of the young defendant. The bench comprises either a District Judge or three specially trained magistrates. While most cases are heard here, very serious offences (such as murder) will start in the Youth Court before being sent to the Crown Court for trial.

items: Deals with offenders aged 10-17.
Aims to prevent re-offending and considers the welfare of the child.
Less formal procedure and setting than adult courts.
Closed to the public with automatic reporting restrictions.
Serious ('grave') crimes are passed to the Crown Court.

sections.content: A Referral Order is a sentence specifically for young offenders appearing in court for the first time and pleading guilty. It is the standard sentence in such cases, unless the offence is so minor it warrants a discharge or so serious it requires custody. The offender is 'referred' to a Youth Offender Panel, composed of community volunteers and a member of the Youth Offending Team. The panel, along with the offender and their parents/guardians, agrees on a 'contract' of activities for a period of 3 to 12 months. This contract aims to repair the harm caused (restorative justice) and address the causes of the offending behaviour, for example through victim apology letters or attending programmes on substance misuse.

items: Mandatory for most first-time guilty pleas.
Involves a Youth Offender Panel, not a judge.
Focuses on restorative justice and preventing re-offending.
Results in a 'contract' of activities lasting 3-12 months.
If the contract is completed, the conviction becomes 'spent'.

sections.content: The Youth Rehabilitation Order (YRO), introduced by the Criminal Justice and Immigration Act 2008, is a flexible community sentence. It can be imposed for any offence for which imprisonment is not fixed by law. A judge in the Youth Court can create a bespoke sentence by choosing from a menu of 18 different requirements to attach to the order. This allows the sentence to be tailored to the individual's needs and the specific offence. Requirements can include a supervision requirement, a curfew, an exclusion requirement, unpaid work (for 16-17 year olds), or participation in specific programmes addressing issues like anger management. The YRO provides a step up from a Referral Order and is used for more serious or persistent offenders.

items: A flexible community sentence for offenders aged 10-17.
A court can attach one or more of 18 possible requirements.
Tailored to the specific circumstances of the offender and the crime.
Examples include supervision, curfew, and activity requirements.
More serious than a Referral Order, used for repeat or more serious offenders.

sections.content: In problem questions, be precise. Don't just say 'community sentence'. If it's a first-time offender with a guilty plea, the likely sentence is a Referral Order. For a repeat offender or a more serious crime not warranting custody, discuss the YRO and suggest 2-3 specific, relevant requirements from the list of 18.
Custodial Sentences: Detention and Training Orders (DTOs)

For the most serious or persistent young offenders, a custodial sentence may be necessary. The main custodial sentence for those aged 12-17 is the Detention and Training Order (DTO). A DTO can last from 4 months to 2 years. The unique feature of a DTO is its structure: the first half is served in custody in a secure children's home, secure training centre, or young offender institution. The second half is served in the community under the supervision of the Youth Offending Team. This ensures a structured transition back into society and continues the focus on rehabilitation. For the gravest crimes (e.g., murder, manslaughter), young offenders can receive longer sentences of detention under sections 90 and 91 of the Powers of Criminal Courts (Sentencing) Act 2000.

items: A DTO is the main custodial sentence for offenders aged 12-17.
It lasts between 4 and 24 months.
Half the sentence is served in custody, half in the community under supervision.
Aims to provide training and education and ensure a structured release.
Longer-term detention is available for 'grave crimes' under PCC(S)A 2000.

sections.question: Tariq, aged 15, pleads guilty in youth court to his first offence — shop theft worth £40. Analyse available sentencing options and the welfare principle. [10 marks]
sections.solution:
**1. Jurisdiction:** Tariq is **15** — youth court has jurisdiction. Age of criminal responsibility is **10** — Tariq is within the system.

**2. Welfare principle:** Under the **Children Act 1989**, the court must have regard to Tariq's **welfare**. Youth sentencing prioritises **rehabilitation** over retribution — contrast with adult framework (2.3.1).

**3. Referral order:** For a **first offence** where Tariq **pleads guilty**, a **referral order** is the **presumed starting point** — youth offender panel agrees a **contract** including reparation to victim and intervention programme. Designed to prevent reoffending without criminalising unnecessarily.

**4. Youth rehabilitation order (YRO):** Available if referral order unsuitable (e.g. not first offence or more serious). For £40 theft, YRO would be **disproportionate** if referral order available.

**5. DTO (custody):** **Detention and training order** reserved for **serious or persistent** offending — completely inappropriate for first-time low-value theft.

**6. Fine/discharge:** Youth court can impose **fine** (parent may pay) or **absolute/conditional discharge**, but referral order normally applies for first guilty plea.

**Conclusion:** **Referral order** is the most appropriate disposal — addresses **rehabilitation**, **reparation**, and **welfare** without custodial sentence.

faq.q: Are all offenders under 18 tried in the Youth Court?
faq.a: No. While the vast majority are, there are exceptions. Firstly, if a young person is charged jointly with an adult, they may be tried together in the adult Magistrates' Court or Crown Court. Secondly, for 'grave crimes' like murder, manslaughter, or other serious offences, the case will be heard in the Crown Court before a judge and jury, although sentencing will still reflect the offender's age.

faq.q: Is the youth justice system just a 'soft option' that lets young people get away with crime?
faq.a: This is a common misconception. The focus of the youth justice system is on preventing re-offending, which evidence suggests is more effective than purely punitive measures. Sentences like Referral Orders and YROs require active participation and commitment from the young person to address their behaviour and repair harm. A DTO is a custodial sentence and a significant deprivation of liberty. The system is designed to be different, not necessarily 'softer', focusing on what works to reduce future crime.

faq.q: Can a child under 10 be held criminally responsible?
faq.a: No. The age of criminal responsibility in England and Wales is 10. Below this age, a child is deemed 'doli incapax', meaning they are considered incapable of forming the necessary criminal intent (mens rea). While they cannot be prosecuted, if their behaviour is a serious concern, other agencies like social services may become involved under civil or family law procedures to ensure the child's welfare and public safety.

comparisonTable.caption: Comparison of Youth Court and Adult Magistrates' Court

cells: Specially trained magistrates (usually 3) or a District Judge.
Lay magistrates (usually 3) or a District Judge.
Less formal. No wigs or gowns. Defendant sits with parents.
More formal. Traditional court layout. Wigs and gowns not worn but more formal language and procedure.
Private. The general public is excluded.
Public. The court is generally open to the public.
Automatic anonymity for the defendant (s.49 Children and Young Persons Act 1933).
No automatic anonymity. Defendants can be named unless a specific reporting restriction is imposed.
To prevent offending and have regard for the welfare of the young person.
Multiple aims including punishment, deterrence, rehabilitation, public protection and reparation (s.142 CJA 2003).
